results.possibleIdentity: Robert 'Bob' Whitman (1935–2024) was a prominent American multimedia and performance artist, best known as a pioneer of the 'Happenings' movement in the late 1950s and 1960s. He was a co-founder of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) in 1966 alongside artist Robert Rauschenberg and engineers Billy Klüver and Fred Waldhauer. His work frequently bridged the gap between art, science, and technology, incorporating film, projections, lasers, and telecommunications. Notable early works include 'American Moon' (1960) and 'Prune Flat' (1965). During the late 1960s and early 1970s, he was a key figure in major E.A.T. projects, such as the '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering' (1966) and the design of the Pepsi Pavilion for Expo '70 in Osaka, where he spearheaded the concept of a large-scale spherical mirror. In the 1970s, he developed innovative communication projects like 'News' (1972) and the 'Telex: Q&A' (1971), which explored the use of payphones and telex equipment as artistic mediums. He was educated at Rutgers University and Columbia University and was long represented by Pace Gallery.

results.possibleIdentity: Nancy Larue Kendall (often cited as Nancy Kendall) is an experimental artist and filmmaker active in the New York City avant-garde and feminist art scenes during the late 1960s and 1970s. She was a founding member of Women/Artist/Filmmakers, Inc. (W/A/F), a pioneering collective active from 1974 to 1981 that included notable artists such as Maria Lassnig, Carolee Schneemann, and Martha Edelheit. The group was notably the first all-women organization to receive funding from the National Endowment for the Arts (NEA) to produce art films. Kendall is best known for her 16mm experimental film 'Almira 38' (1970), a black-and-white sound work featuring dancer Gay Delanghe, which is described as a 'dance poem' and a tribute to a woman seeking freedom. Her work was featured in the landmark 1973 exhibition 'Women Choose Women' at the New York Cultural Center and was screened at major venues like the Parrish Art Museum and the Petzel Gallery in recent years. She was part of the interdisciplinary community associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement through her collaborations within the W/A/F collective, where members frequently explored the intersection of technology and art. In her personal life, she was likely married to the artist and contractor Joseph Harry Hubbard (1933–2015), with whom she resided on Staten Island before moving to New Hampshire.

results.possibleIdentity: Ultra Violet (born Isabelle Collin Dufresne, 1935–2014) was a French-American artist, author, and actress who was a prominent figure in the 1960s and 1970s New York avant-garde. Originally a muse and studio assistant to Salvador Dalí in the 1950s, she joined Andy Warhol’s Factory in 1963 and became one of his most famous 'superstars.' She adopted the name Ultra Violet at Warhol's suggestion or after reading a New York Times article about light in outer space. During the late 1960s, she was an active participant in Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit founded by Robert Rauschenberg and Billy Klüver to bridge the gap between artists and engineers; archival records from the Getty Research Institute list her as a member involved in the organization's 'Technical Services Program' around 1968–1969. Beyond her film roles in Warhol's underground cinema and mainstream movies like 'Midnight Cowboy' (1969), she was a dedicated plastic artist whose later work explored themes of light, mirrors, and spirituality. In 1988, she published her well-known autobiography, 'Famous for 15 Minutes: My Years with Andy Warhol.' After a near-death experience in 1973, she underwent a spiritual transformation and became a member of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints in 1981.

results.possibleIdentity: Giselle Mauer (born 1930) is a Romanian-born sculptor who was active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. She is documented as a participant in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, specifically contributing to the landmark exhibition 'Some More Beginnings: An Exhibition of Submitted Works Involving Technical Materials and Processes' held at the Brooklyn Museum from November 1968 to February 1969. In her E.A.T. activities, she often collaborated with artist Ben-Ami Kadish (1923–2012). Mauer's work in the early 1970s included modern metal sculpture, such as a piece unveiled at the Hillel Center in East Brunswick, New Jersey, in 1972. She was also involved in community-oriented art projects, including organizing international children's art displays in the mid-1960s.
